title: PUBLIC SERVICE COUNTER HOURS OF OPERATION CHANGE
content:
For almost the last decade, the PMPD was able to offer expanded hours with the Public Safety Building open past 4 PM to 7 PM Monday to Friday.  As the PMPD continues to optimize internal processes, the PMPD made the difficult decision to shift resourcing into other areas of the organization to better support operations and improve public safety.  The emergency telephone just outside the main front doors of the Public Safety Building remains available to the public 24/7.  The PMPD has seen significant improvements in the ability of EComm to provide reduced wait times for non-emergency calls and emergency calls remain well within target answer times.
Chief Constable Fleugel noted, “coupled with the improvements at EComm, we have successfully implemented online crime reporting and transitioned to an online Police Information Check system several years ago with good success.  We are just not seeing the foot traffic at to the police office in the evening hours to justify the use of precious salary dollars at those hours any longer.  Our core business hours remain unchanged.”
Released by:
Constable Sam Zacharias, #122
Media Relations Officer, Port Moody Police Department

title: Port Moody Police phase in body-worn cameras
content:
-These cameras are mounted to officers’ uniforms and will record interactions for evidentiary and accountability purposes.
-Officers will activate their cameras as soon as practical during interactions with the public and will also inform the person being recorded as soon as practical.
-Officers will not continuously record on their cameras if they are not responding to a specific incident. There may be times when officers will use discretion not to record interactions that are sensitive in nature including in healthcare settings, places of worship and in courtrooms.
“The introduction of body-worn cameras is an important milestone for our department and our community. These cameras provide an impartial record of interactions, enhance transparency and accountability and help strengthen public trust. The PMPD consulted the community for our 2024-2026 Strategic Plan and learned that citizens were supportive of introducing this technology and we are now ready to do that. Body-worn cameras will support our officers by capturing critical evidence and providing greater clarity during complex incidents. Ultimately, body-worn cameras are another tool that helps us deliver professional, modern, and effective policing to the citizens of Port Moody." – Chief Constable David Fleugel.
The Port Moody Police Department anticipates a full rollout of body-worn cameras to frontline officers by late 2026.
Released by:
Constable Sam Zacharias #122

title: THREE SUSPECTS CAUGHT CLIMBING TOWER CRANE
content:
A trio of teenage boys were arrested Thursday evening after allegedly climbing a tower crane.
On May 7, 2026 just before 11 p.m., frontline officers were called to a fully fenced construction site in the 2300 block of St Johns Street for a report of trespassers who were actively climbing a tower crane.
Officers arrived within several minutes and observed the suspects who had ascended the crane. The trio surrendered and were arrested without incident – later released to guardians as investigators consider charge recommendation.
“It should go without saying, these stunts are not only illegal but dangerous,” states Constable Sam Zacharias, adding that “construction sites like this one are live monitored around-the-clock with video and motion sensors – police will always be called in instances of trespassers.”

title: WANTED MAN ARRESTED AFTER ALLEGED CRIME SPREE
content:
An alert citizen helped Port Moody Police capture a wanted man after an alleged property crime spree.
On April 26, 2026, around 9:45 p.m., Port Moody Police officers were called to a report of a possible theft in the Ioco Road corridor of Port Moody. The caller reported seeing a man rummaging through multiple vehicles in the residential neighbourhood. Officers patrolled the area and were not able to locate the suspect initially, however, did obtain surveillance footage of the incident. Approximately an hour later, the man was located by officers several kilometers away and arrested without incident. Of note, the man was also wanted on an outstanding arrest warrant.
The
B.C. Prosecution Service
has approved two counts of mischief and five counts of breaching a release order against 31-year-old Marc Smaaslet. Smaaslet remains in custody at this time and has his next court appearance later this morning.
“Incidents like these are a great reminder of the vital role the public plays in helping us curb crime in our community,” states Constable Sam Zacharias. “We thank the diligent witness who contacted us right away and helped advance our investigation – leading to the eventual arrest of the accused.”

title: DRIVER FLEES, CRASHES WITH PASSENGER IN TRUNK
content:
Port Moody Police investigated an unusual collision Saturday morning.
On April 18, 2026 just after 4 a.m., a patrol officer spotted a heavily occupied Honda sedan with an ‘N’ decal travelling on Clarke Road. The officer turned his police vehicle around to attempt to stop the vehicle and the driver of the Honda fled onto a side street. A short time later, the officer located the vehicle which had crashed into the front yard of a residential home.
In addition to the driver, 5 passengers were located inside of the vehicle. One passenger was inside of the vehicle’s trunk at the time of the collision.
The 19-year-old class 7 driver did not pass the roadside alcohol screening test and now faces multiple Motor Vehicle Act charges and a driving prohibition. All of the parties were assessed by firefighters at scene.
“Fortunately, there were no serious injuries reported as a result of this collision,” states Constable Sam Zacharias, adding that “this vehicle had more occupants than seats and it certainly could have been a far worse outcome. We would like to remind the public to always plan a safe ride home – there are so many options available including public transit and rideshares.”

title: UNSAFE TRUCK PULLED FROM ROADWAY
content:
Port Moody Police officers observed a startling incident Wednesday morning when a commercial vehicle lost two wheels while travelling on the highway.
On March 11, 2026 around 10:30 a.m., frontline officers were travelling near a dump truck entering Port Moody on westbound Barnet Highway. Officers observed the dump truck’s two rear wheels and a brake drum become dislodged – hurling into other lanes of traffic. Officers were able to safely avoid the objects but one of the wheels did strike a bystander’s vehicle causing damage. It was determined the lug nuts were not properly fastened and the vehicle was removed from the road for a complete inspection. The driver was also issued a violation ticket by officers.
“We routinely conduct commercial vehicle enforcement in Port Moody and incidents like these reinforce the importance of this type of enforcement,” states Constable Sam Zacharias, adding that “this could have been much worse and it is fortunate there were no injuries as a result.”
Constable Sam Zacharias, #122
